tagline: Calculate electricity cost from kilowatt-hours and rate
keywords: kwh cost calculator, electricity cost, kilowatt hour price, energy cost
calcFn: var total = kwh * rate; document.getElementById('totalCost').textContent = dollar(total); document.getElementById('dailyCost').textContent = dollar(total / 30); document.getElementById('annualCost').textContent = dollar(total * 12);
formulas: Cost = kWh × Rate per kWh
US average residential rate: ~$0.14/kWh
Average household uses ~900 kWh/month

faq.question: What is a kWh?
faq.answer: A kilowatt-hour is a unit of energy equal to using 1,000 watts for one hour. A 100W light bulb running for 10 hours uses 1 kWh. It's how utility companies measure and bill electricity usage.

faq.question: What is the average electricity rate?
faq.answer: The US average is about $0.14/kWh, ranging from $0.09 (Louisiana) to $0.36 (Hawaii). Rates vary by state, utility, time of day (TOU pricing), and season.

faq.question: How can I reduce my kWh usage?
faq.answer: Top strategies: switch to LED bulbs (75% savings), upgrade to efficient appliances, use a smart thermostat (10-15% heating/cooling savings), seal air leaks, and run heavy loads during off-peak hours.

tagline: Calculate annual energy cost for any appliance
keywords: appliance energy calculator, appliance electricity cost, energy use calculator, watt cost
calcFn: var dailyKwh = (watts * hoursDay) / 1000; var monthly = dailyKwh * 30; var daily = dailyKwh * rate; document.getElementById('dailyCost').textContent = dollar(daily); document.getElementById('monthlyCost').textContent = dollar(daily * 30); document.getElementById('annualCost').textContent = dollar(daily * 365); document.getElementById('kwhMonth').textContent = fmt(monthly, 1) + ' kWh';
formulas: Daily kWh = (Watts × Hours/Day) ÷ 1,000
Daily Cost = Daily kWh × Rate
Annual Cost = Daily Cost × 365

faq.question: What appliances use the most electricity?
faq.answer: HVAC (heating/cooling): 40-50% of bills. Water heater: 15-20%. Washer/dryer: 10%. Refrigerator: 5-8%. Oven/stove: 3-5%. LED lights are minimal. Space heaters are expensive per-hour (1,500W).

faq.question: How many watts does a refrigerator use?
faq.answer: Modern refrigerators use 100-400W while running, but cycle on and off. Average annual usage is 400-600 kWh/year ($55-85/year). Older models can use 2-3× more.

faq.question: How do I find my appliance's wattage?
faq.answer: Check the label or plate on the back/bottom of the appliance. It lists wattage (W) or amps (A) × volts (V) = watts. You can also use a Kill-A-Watt meter plugged into the outlet for real-time measurement.

tagline: Calculate savings from switching to LED bulbs
keywords: led savings calculator, led vs incandescent, light bulb savings, led cost savings
calcFn: var oldKwh = (oldWatts * numBulbs * hoursDay * 365) / 1000; var newKwh = (ledWatts * numBulbs * hoursDay * 365) / 1000; var saved = oldKwh - newKwh; var savings = saved * rate; var bulbCost = numBulbs * 3; var payback = bulbCost / (savings / 12); document.getElementById('annualSavings').textContent = dollar(savings); document.getElementById('monthlySavings').textContent = dollar(savings / 12); document.getElementById('kwhSaved').textContent = fmt(saved, 0) + ' kWh'; document.getElementById('payback').textContent = fmt(payback, 1) + ' months';
formulas: kWh saved = (Old watts − LED watts) × bulbs × hours × 365 ÷ 1,000
Annual savings = kWh saved × rate
LED bulbs use 75-85% less energy than incandescent

faq.question: How much can LED bulbs save?
faq.answer: Replacing 20 incandescent bulbs (60W) with LEDs (9W) saves about $150-200/year. LEDs use 75-85% less energy and last 15-25× longer. Most households see payback within 2-3 months.

faq.question: Do LED bulbs really last 25,000 hours?
faq.answer: Quality LEDs from major brands typically last 25,000-50,000 hours — that's 14-28 years at 5 hours/day. Cheap LEDs may fail sooner. Heat is the main enemy of LED lifespan, so ensure adequate ventilation.

faq.question: What LED wattage equals a 60W incandescent?
faq.answer: An 8-10W LED produces the same brightness as a 60W incandescent (~800 lumens). For 100W equivalent, use 15-17W LED. For 40W, use 5-6W LED. Always compare lumens, not watts.

tagline: Compare heat pump costs vs traditional heating
keywords: heat pump savings calculator, heat pump vs furnace, heat pump cost comparison, heat pump roi
calcFn: var effMap = {gas: 0.80, oil: 0.85, electric: 1.0, propane: 0.80}; var eff = effMap[fuelType] || 0.80; var heatNeeded = heatingCost * eff; var hpElecCost = (heatNeeded / hpCOP) * (elecRate / 0.034); if (fuelType === 'electric') { hpElecCost = heatingCost / hpCOP; } var savings = heatingCost - hpElecCost; var installCost = 8000; var payback = savings > 0 ? installCost / savings : 999; document.getElementById('hpAnnual').textContent = dollar(Math.max(0, hpElecCost)); document.getElementById('annualSavings').textContent = dollar(Math.max(0, savings)); document.getElementById('payback').textContent = savings > 0 ? fmt(payback, 1) + ' years (on ~$8K install)' : 'May not save with current rates';
formulas: Heat pump delivers COP × electricity input as heat
COP 3.0 means 300% efficient — 1 kWh electricity → 3 kWh heat
Savings = Current heating cost − Heat pump running cost
Typical install: $4,000-12,000 after federal tax credits

faq.question: What is heat pump COP?
faq.answer: Coefficient of Performance (COP) measures efficiency. COP 3.0 means for every 1 kWh of electricity, the pump delivers 3 kWh of heat. Modern heat pumps achieve COP 2.5-4.5 depending on outdoor temperature.

faq.question: Do heat pumps work in cold climates?
faq.answer: Modern cold-climate heat pumps work efficiently down to -15°F. Brands like Mitsubishi Hyper-Heat and Daikin maintain heating capacity to -13°F. Below their rated temperature, a backup heat source may be needed.

faq.question: How much does a heat pump cost to install?
faq.answer: Mini-split: $3,000-8,000. Central ducted: $8,000-15,000. After the 30% federal tax credit (through 2032), costs drop significantly. Many states offer additional rebates of $1,000-5,000.

tagline: Calculate recommended insulation R-value by climate zone
keywords: r value calculator, insulation calculator, insulation r value, attic insulation depth
calcFn: var recs = {'1':{attic:30,wall:13,floor:13,basement:11},'2':{attic:38,wall:13,floor:13,basement:11},'3':{attic:38,wall:13,floor:19,basement:11},'4':{attic:49,wall:13,floor:25,basement:11},'5':{attic:49,wall:20,floor:25,basement:15},'6':{attic:60,wall:20,floor:30,basement:15},'7':{attic:60,wall:21,floor:30,basement:15}}; var z = recs[zone] || recs['4']; var R = z[area] || 30; var fg = R / 3.2; var cel = R / 3.7; var foam = R / 6.5; document.getElementById('rValue').textContent = 'R-' + R; document.getElementById('fgDepth').textContent = fmt(fg, 1) + ' inches'; document.getElementById('cellDepth').textContent = fmt(cel, 1) + ' inches'; document.getElementById('foamDepth').textContent = fmt(foam, 1) + ' inches';
formulas: R-value per inch: Fiberglass batts ~3.2, Cellulose ~3.7, Spray foam ~6.5
Depth needed = Target R-value ÷ R-per-inch
Higher R-value = better insulation = lower heating/cooling costs

faq.question: What R-value do I need?
faq.answer: It depends on climate zone and location. Attics: R-30 to R-60. Walls: R-13 to R-21. Floors: R-13 to R-30. Colder climates need higher values. Check your zone at energystar.gov.

faq.question: What is R-value?
faq.answer: R-value measures thermal resistance — how well material resists heat flow. Higher R-value = better insulation. R-38 is standard for attics in moderate climates. The value is per inch of material thickness.

faq.question: Which insulation type is best?
faq.answer: Spray foam: best R-value per inch (R-6.5) and air sealing, but most expensive. Cellulose: good value, eco-friendly (recycled paper), great for retrofits. Fiberglass batts: cheapest, easy DIY, but must be installed without gaps.

tagline: Estimate your monthly electric bill from usage
keywords: electricity bill calculator, electric bill estimator, monthly electric cost, utility bill calculator
calcFn: var kwhPerSqft = {south: 0.85, north: 0.60, mid: 0.65, west: 0.50}; var factor = kwhPerSqft[region] || 0.65; var kwh = sqft * factor; var bill = kwh * rate; document.getElementById('estKwh').textContent = fmt(kwh, 0) + ' kWh'; document.getElementById('estBill').textContent = dollar(bill); document.getElementById('annual').textContent = dollar(bill * 12);
formulas: Estimated kWh = Square Footage × Regional Factor
South: ~0.85 kWh/sqft, North: ~0.60, Mid-Atlantic: ~0.65, West: ~0.50
Monthly Bill = kWh × Rate

faq.question: What is the average electric bill?
faq.answer: US average is about $125-150/month (~900 kWh). Southern states average $150-170 (AC costs). Northeast: $120-140. Pacific states: $100-120. Bills vary widely by home size, efficiency, and local rates.

faq.question: Why is my electric bill so high?
faq.answer: Common culprits: old HVAC system, poor insulation, electric water heater, space heaters, pool pump, old refrigerator, or phantom loads. Get a home energy audit to identify the biggest opportunities.

faq.question: How can I lower my electric bill?
faq.answer: Top 5 actions: 1) Smart thermostat (save 10-15%), 2) LED bulbs everywhere, 3) Seal air leaks, 4) Energy Star appliances, 5) Lower water heater to 120°F. Combined, these can cut bills 20-30%.

tagline: Convert watts to kWh and calculate energy costs
keywords: power consumption calculator, watts to kwh, energy consumption, watt hour calculator
calcFn: var kwh = (watts * hours) / 1000; var cost = kwh * rate; document.getElementById('kwhUsed').textContent = fmt(kwh, 2) + ' kWh'; document.getElementById('cost').textContent = dollar(cost); document.getElementById('monthly').textContent = dollar(cost * 30) + '/month';
formulas: kWh = Watts × Hours ÷ 1,000
Cost = kWh × Rate per kWh
1 kW for 1 hour = 1 kWh

faq.question: What is the difference between watts and kWh?
faq.answer: Watts measure power (rate of energy use). kWh measure energy (total consumption over time). A 100W bulb uses 100 watts of power. Running it for 10 hours uses 1 kWh of energy. You pay for kWh, not watts.

faq.question: How do I convert watts to kWh?
faq.answer: Multiply watts by hours, then divide by 1,000. Example: 1,500W heater for 3 hours = 1,500 × 3 ÷ 1,000 = 4.5 kWh. At $0.14/kWh, that costs $0.63.

faq.question: How many watts is 1 amp?
faq.answer: Watts = Amps × Volts. In the US (120V): 1 amp = 120 watts. A 15-amp circuit can handle 1,800 watts max (15A × 120V). A 20-amp circuit: 2,400 watts max.

tagline: Calculate your carbon footprint and offset cost
keywords: carbon offset calculator, carbon footprint, co2 emissions calculator, carbon neutral
calcFn: var driveCO2 = (driving / mpg) * 8.887 / 1000; var flightCO2 = flights * 1.6; var elecCO2 = (homeKwh * 12 * 0.855) / 2205; var total = driveCO2 + flightCO2 + elecCO2; var cost = total * 15; document.getElementById('totalTons').textContent = fmt(total, 1) + ' tons'; document.getElementById('offsetCost').textContent = dollar(cost) + ' at ~$15/ton'; document.getElementById('driving_co2').textContent = fmt(driveCO2, 1) + ' tons'; document.getElementById('flight_co2').textContent = fmt(flightCO2, 1) + ' tons';
formulas: Driving CO₂ = (miles ÷ MPG) × 8.887 kg/gallon ÷ 1,000
Flight CO₂ ≈ 1.6 metric tons per round-trip domestic flight
Electric CO₂ = kWh × 0.855 lbs/kWh ÷ 2,205 (US grid average)
Offset cost: ~$10-20 per metric ton

faq.question: What is a carbon offset?
faq.answer: A carbon offset is a reduction in CO₂ emissions made to compensate for emissions elsewhere. You fund projects like reforestation, renewable energy, or methane capture that remove or prevent greenhouse gas emissions.

faq.question: What is the average American's carbon footprint?
faq.answer: About 16 metric tons of CO₂ per year — one of the highest in the world. The global average is about 4 tons. To limit warming to 1.5°C, we need to reach about 2 tons per person by 2050.

faq.question: Do carbon offsets actually work?
faq.answer: Quality varies enormously. Look for verified offsets (Gold Standard, VCS, ACR certified). Direct air capture and avoided deforestation are among the most credible. Cheap offsets ($5/ton) are often questionable. Budget $15-25/ton for quality.

tagline: Calculate apparent temperature from heat and humidity
keywords: heat index calculator, feels like temperature, apparent temperature, heat humidity calculator
calcFn: var T = tempF; var R = rh; var HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523*T + 10.14333127*R - 0.22475541*T*R - 0.00683783*T*T - 0.05481717*R*R + 0.00122874*T*T*R + 0.00085282*T*R*R - 0.00000199*T*T*R*R; if (R < 13 && T > 80 && T < 112) HI -= ((13-R)/4)*Math.sqrt((17-Math.abs(T-95))/17); if (R > 85 && T > 80 && T < 87) HI += ((R-85)/10)*((87-T)/5); if (HI < T) HI = T; var danger = HI >= 130 ? 'Extreme Danger' : HI >= 105 ? 'Danger' : HI >= 90 ? 'Extreme Caution' : HI >= 80 ? 'Caution' : 'Safe'; var adv = HI >= 105 ? 'Heat stroke likely with prolonged exposure. Stay indoors.' : HI >= 90 ? 'Heat exhaustion possible. Limit outdoor activity.' : 'Normal outdoor activity OK. Stay hydrated.'; document.getElementById('heatIndex').textContent = fmt(HI, 0) + '°F'; document.getElementById('danger').textContent = danger; document.getElementById('advisory').textContent = adv;
formulas: Rothfusz regression equation (NWS formula)
Adjustments applied for low humidity + high temp and high humidity + moderate temp
Heat index only valid when air temp ≥ 80°F and humidity ≥ 40%

faq.question: What is heat index?
faq.answer: Heat index (or 'feels like' temperature) combines air temperature and humidity to show how hot it actually feels. At 95°F with 60% humidity, the heat index is about 114°F because your body can't cool itself efficiently through sweating.

faq.question: At what heat index is it dangerous?
faq.answer: Caution: 80-90°F. Extreme Caution: 90-105°F (heat exhaustion possible). Danger: 105-130°F (heat stroke likely). Extreme Danger: 130°F+ (heat stroke imminent). These thresholds are for prolonged exposure.

faq.question: Why does humidity make heat worse?
faq.answer: Your body cools itself by evaporating sweat. High humidity slows evaporation, trapping heat. At 100°F with 10% humidity (desert), sweat evaporates quickly. At 100°F with 70% humidity, sweat can't evaporate and body temperature rises dangerously.

tagline: Assess health impact from AQI levels
keywords: aqi calculator, air quality health, air quality index, pollution health impact
calcFn: var cat, risk, rec; if (aqi <= 50) { cat = 'Good'; risk = 'None'; rec = 'Air quality is satisfactory. Enjoy outdoor activities.'; } else if (aqi <= 100) { cat = 'Moderate'; risk = sensitive === 'yes' ? 'Low-Moderate' : 'Low'; rec = sensitive === 'yes' ? 'Unusually sensitive people should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion.' : 'Acceptable for most people. Sensitive groups may have mild effects.'; } else if (aqi <= 150) { cat = 'Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups'; risk = sensitive === 'yes' ? 'Moderate-High' : 'Low-Moderate'; rec = sensitive === 'yes' ? 'Avoid prolonged outdoor exertion. Keep rescue inhaler handy.' : 'Most people OK. Reduce prolonged outdoor exertion if you feel symptoms.'; } else if (aqi <= 200) { cat = 'Unhealthy'; risk = sensitive === 'yes' ? 'High' : 'Moderate'; rec = 'Everyone should reduce outdoor exertion. Sensitive groups should stay indoors.'; } else if (aqi <= 300) { cat = 'Very Unhealthy'; risk = 'High'; rec = 'Avoid all outdoor exertion. Keep windows closed. Use air purifier indoors.'; } else { cat = 'Hazardous'; risk = 'Very High'; rec = 'Stay indoors. Seal windows. Use N95 mask if you must go outside. Seek medical attention for symptoms.'; } document.getElementById('category').textContent = cat; document.getElementById('risk').textContent = risk; document.getElementById('recommendation').textContent = rec;
formulas: AQI 0-50: Good, 51-100: Moderate, 101-150: Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups
151-200: Unhealthy, 201-300: Very Unhealthy, 301-500: Hazardous
Sensitive groups: children, elderly, asthma, heart/lung disease

faq.question: What is AQI?
faq.answer: Air Quality Index (AQI) is a scale from 0-500 measuring air pollution levels. It accounts for PM2.5, PM10, ozone, NO₂, SO₂, and CO. AQI above 100 is considered unhealthy for sensitive groups.

faq.question: What is PM2.5?
faq.answer: PM2.5 are fine particles less than 2.5 micrometers in diameter — small enough to enter your lungs and bloodstream. Sources include wildfire smoke, vehicle exhaust, and industrial emissions. It's the most health-relevant pollutant in AQI.

faq.question: Do air purifiers help with poor AQI?
faq.answer: Yes, HEPA air purifiers can reduce indoor PM2.5 by 50-80%. Run them on high during poor AQI days with windows closed. A $100-200 HEPA purifier is one of the best health investments for areas with frequent air quality issues.

tagline: Estimate flood risk from projected sea level rise
keywords: sea level rise calculator, coastal flooding, climate change flood risk, sea level projection
calcFn: var maxRise = {low: 1, moderate: 2, high: 4, extreme: 6.5}; var max = maxRise[scenario] || 2; var yearsFrom2020 = yearTarget - 2020; var rise = max * (yearsFrom2020 / 80); var margin = elevation - rise; var risk = margin <= 0 ? 'Extreme — Below projected sea level' : margin <= 3 ? 'High — Within storm surge range' : margin <= 6 ? 'Moderate — Vulnerable to major storms' : 'Low — Adequate elevation buffer'; document.getElementById('projectedRise').textContent = fmt(rise, 1) + ' feet'; document.getElementById('margin').textContent = fmt(margin, 1) + ' feet remaining'; document.getElementById('riskLevel').textContent = risk;
formulas: Rise projection scaled linearly from 2020 baseline to 2100 target
NOAA scenarios: Low (1ft), Intermediate (2ft), High (4ft), Extreme (6.5ft) by 2100
Storm surge adds 3-15+ feet on top of sea level during hurricanes

faq.question: How much will sea levels rise?
faq.answer: NOAA projects 1-6.5 feet of rise by 2100 depending on emissions. The current rate is ~3.6mm/year and accelerating. Even under best-case scenarios, at least 1 foot of rise is locked in from ice already melting.

faq.question: What areas are most at risk?
faq.answer: South Florida, Louisiana Gulf Coast, Chesapeake Bay, NYC metro, and Pacific islands face the highest risk. Any coastal area below 10 feet elevation should plan for eventual flooding impacts.

faq.question: Does this account for storm surge?
faq.answer: No — this shows baseline sea level only. Hurricane storm surge adds 3-15+ feet. A Category 3 hurricane with 3 feet of sea level rise could push water 18+ feet above current sea level in vulnerable areas.

tagline: Calculate dew point from temperature and humidity
keywords: dew point calculator, dew point temperature, humidity calculator, condensation point
calcFn: var T = (tempF - 32) * 5/9; var a = 17.271; var b = 237.7; var gamma = (a * T)/(b + T) + Math.log(rh/100); var dpC = (b * gamma)/(a - gamma); var dpF = dpC * 9/5 + 32; var spread = tempF - dpF; var comfort = dpF >= 75 ? 'Extremely Uncomfortable — Oppressive' : dpF >= 70 ? 'Very Humid — Uncomfortable' : dpF >= 65 ? 'Humid — Somewhat Uncomfortable' : dpF >= 60 ? 'Comfortable — Pleasant' : dpF >= 55 ? 'Dry — Very Comfortable' : 'Very Dry — Excellent'; document.getElementById('dewPoint').textContent = fmt(dpF, 1) + '°F (' + fmt(dpC, 1) + '°C)'; document.getElementById('comfort').textContent = comfort; document.getElementById('spreadF').textContent = fmt(spread, 1) + '°F';
formulas: Magnus formula: γ = (aT)/(b+T) + ln(RH/100)
Dew Point = (b × γ)/(a − γ) where a=17.271, b=237.7°C
Smaller spread = closer to saturation (fog/rain more likely)

faq.question: What is dew point?
faq.answer: Dew point is the temperature at which air becomes saturated and water condenses. It's a better measure of humidity comfort than relative humidity. Dew point above 65°F feels humid, above 70°F is uncomfortable, above 75°F is oppressive.

faq.question: How is dew point different from humidity?
faq.answer: Relative humidity is a percentage that changes with temperature — 50% RH at 90°F is muggy, but 50% RH at 40°F is dry. Dew point is an absolute measure: 70°F dew point always feels the same regardless of air temperature.

faq.question: Why does dew point matter for comfort?
faq.answer: Your body cools by evaporating sweat. When dew point is high (65°F+), the air holds so much moisture that sweat can't evaporate efficiently, making you feel hot and sticky even at moderate temperatures.

tagline: Calculate WBGT heat stress index for outdoor work
keywords: heat stress calculator, wbgt calculator, outdoor worker heat, heat illness prevention
calcFn: var T = tempF; var sunAdd = {full: 7, partial: 3, shade: 0}; var sa = sunAdd[sunExposure] || 0; var wbgtEst = 0.567 * T + 0.393 * (humidity / 100 * 6.105 * Math.exp(17.27 * ((T-32)*5/9) / (237.7 + (T-32)*5/9))) + 3.94 + sa; var wbgtF = wbgtEst; var thresholds = {light: 86, moderate: 82, heavy: 77}; var thresh = thresholds[workload] || 82; var risk = wbgtF >= thresh + 10 ? 'Extreme — Stop work' : wbgtF >= thresh + 5 ? 'High — 15 min work, 45 min rest' : wbgtF >= thresh ? 'Moderate — 30 min work, 30 min rest' : 'Low — Normal work with hydration'; var wr = wbgtF >= thresh + 10 ? 'Stop outdoor work. Emergency risk.' : wbgtF >= thresh + 5 ? '15 min work / 45 min rest per hour' : wbgtF >= thresh ? '30 min work / 30 min rest per hour' : '50 min work / 10 min rest per hour'; document.getElementById('wbgt').textContent = fmt(wbgtF, 0) + '°F'; document.getElementById('riskLevel').textContent = risk; document.getElementById('workRest').textContent = wr;
formulas: WBGT estimates wet-bulb globe temperature from temp + humidity + sun
Thresholds: Light work 86°F, Moderate 82°F, Heavy 77°F WBGT
OSHA recommends work/rest cycles when WBGT exceeds thresholds

faq.question: What is WBGT?
faq.answer: Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) is the gold standard for measuring heat stress risk. Unlike heat index, it accounts for humidity, wind, sun angle, and cloud cover. OSHA uses WBGT to set outdoor work limits.

faq.question: At what temperature should outdoor work stop?
faq.answer: There's no single temperature. It depends on humidity, sun exposure, and work intensity. OSHA guidelines: rest breaks start at WBGT 77-86°F depending on workload. Above WBGT 90°F, most outdoor work should be limited to essential tasks.

faq.question: How can employers reduce heat stress risk?
faq.answer: Provide water every 15 minutes, shade breaks, gradual acclimatization (2 weeks), light-colored clothing, buddy system, and training on heat illness signs. OSHA requires a heat injury prevention program when temperatures exceed 80°F.

tagline: Calculate rainwater collection from roof area and rainfall
keywords: rainwater harvest calculator, rain barrel calculator, rainwater collection, rain catchment
calcFn: var gal = roofSqFt * (rainfall / 12) * 7.48 * (efficiency / 100); var annual = gal * 12; document.getElementById('gallonsMonth').textContent = fmt(gal, 0) + ' gallons'; document.getElementById('gallonsYear').textContent = fmt(annual, 0) + ' gallons'; document.getElementById('barrelsMonth').textContent = fmt(gal / 55, 1) + ' barrels';
formulas: Volume = Roof Area × Rainfall Depth × 7.48 gal/cu ft
1 inch of rain on 1,000 sq ft ≈ 623 gallons
85% efficiency accounts for gutter losses and first-flush diversion

faq.question: How much rainwater can I collect?
faq.answer: About 600 gallons per 1,000 sq ft of roof per inch of rain. A 1,500 sq ft roof with 35 inches of annual rainfall can collect about 27,000 gallons per year — enough for a significant portion of landscape irrigation.

faq.question: Is rainwater harvesting legal?
faq.answer: Most US states allow it. Colorado previously restricted it but now allows up to two 55-gallon barrels per household. Some states actively encourage it with tax credits. Check your state and local regulations.

faq.question: What can I use harvested rainwater for?
faq.answer: Garden irrigation is the most common use. With proper filtration and treatment, it can be used for flushing toilets, laundry, and even drinking water. Untreated rainwater should not be used for drinking or cooking.

tagline: Calculate roof snow load from depth and density
keywords: snow load calculator, roof snow weight, snow removal calculator, snow density
calcFn: var densities = {fresh: 5, avg: 10, wet: 15, ice: 30}; var density = densities[snowType] || 10; var psfLoad = (depth / 12) * density; var total = psfLoad * roofSqFt; var risk = psfLoad >= 40 ? 'Critical — Remove snow immediately' : psfLoad >= 25 ? 'High — Consider snow removal' : psfLoad >= 15 ? 'Moderate — Monitor closely' : 'Low — Within normal range'; document.getElementById('psf').textContent = fmt(psfLoad, 1) + ' psf'; document.getElementById('totalWeight').textContent = fmt(total, 0) + ' lbs (' + fmt(total / 2000, 1) + ' tons)'; document.getElementById('riskLevel').textContent = risk;
formulas: Snow Load (psf) = (Depth in ft) × Density (lbs/cu ft)
Fresh snow: ~5 lbs/cu ft, Settled: ~10, Wet: ~15, Ice: ~30
Most roofs designed for 20-40 psf ground snow load

faq.question: How much snow can a roof hold?
faq.answer: Most modern roofs are designed for 20-40 psf snow load. That's about 4 feet of fresh powder or 2 feet of settled snow. Older or flat roofs may have lower limits. If you're concerned, consult a structural engineer.

faq.question: When should I remove snow from my roof?
faq.answer: Consider removal when snow exceeds 2 feet of settled snow, when you see ice dams forming, when the roof is sagging, or when adding wet heavy snow on top of existing accumulation. Use a roof rake from the ground — never climb on a snow-covered roof.

faq.question: How heavy is wet snow?
faq.answer: Wet heavy snow weighs about 15 lbs per cubic foot — 3× heavier than fresh powder. One foot of wet snow on a 1,500 sq ft roof adds about 22,500 lbs (11+ tons). This is why roof collapses often happen during heavy wet snowstorms.

tagline: Calculate safe sun exposure time by skin type
keywords: uv exposure calculator, sun exposure time, sunburn calculator, uv index skin type
calcFn: var baseMins = {1: 67, 2: 100, 3: 200, 4: 300, 5: 400, 6: 500}; var base = baseMins[skinType] || 200; var burnMin = base / uvIndex; var protectedMin = burnMin * spf * 0.5; var cat = uvIndex <= 2 ? 'Low' : uvIndex <= 5 ? 'Moderate' : uvIndex <= 7 ? 'High' : uvIndex <= 10 ? 'Very High' : 'Extreme'; document.getElementById('burnTime').textContent = fmt(burnMin, 0) + ' minutes'; document.getElementById('protectedTime').textContent = fmt(Math.min(protectedMin, 480), 0) + ' minutes (' + fmt(Math.min(protectedMin, 480) / 60, 1) + ' hours)'; document.getElementById('uvCategory').textContent = cat + ' (UV ' + uvIndex + ')'; document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = 'Reapply sunscreen every 2 hours regardless of SPF.';
formulas: Burn time = Skin type base (minutes) ÷ UV Index
Protected time = Burn time × SPF × 0.5 (real-world factor)
Capped at 8 hours — reapply sunscreen every 2 hours regardless
UV Index 1-2: Low, 3-5: Moderate, 6-7: High, 8-10: Very High, 11+: Extreme

faq.question: What UV index is dangerous?
faq.answer: UV 1-2: Low risk. UV 3-5: Moderate, wear sunscreen. UV 6-7: High, reduce midday exposure. UV 8-10: Very High, avoid 10am-4pm sun. UV 11+: Extreme, stay in shade. Most US summer days reach UV 7-10.

faq.question: Does SPF 100 give twice the protection of SPF 50?
faq.answer: No. SPF 30 blocks 97% of UVB, SPF 50 blocks 98%, SPF 100 blocks 99%. The difference is minimal. More important: apply enough (1 oz for full body), reapply every 2 hours, and reapply after swimming/sweating.

faq.question: Can you get sunburned on cloudy days?
faq.answer: Yes. Up to 80% of UV rays penetrate clouds. You can also burn in the shade due to reflected UV from sand (25%), water (10%), concrete (12%), and snow (80%). UV exposure is cumulative throughout the day.
